description: 'General instructions for the Smart Charging application. These instructions apply to all files in the project and should be followed by all developers working on the application.'
applyTo: '**/*'
---

# Smart Charging

## Design Principles

When developing the tool application, you should follow these design principles:
- **KISS** (Keep It Simple, Stupid): The application should be simple and easy to use. Avoid unnecessary complexity and features that do not add value to the user.
- **YAGNI** (You Ain't Gonna Need It): Only implement features that are necessary for the current requirements. Avoid implementing features that may be needed in the future but are not currently required.
- **DRY** (Don't Repeat Yourself): Avoid duplicating code and logic. Instead, create reusable components and functions that can be used throughout the application. This will help to reduce the amount of code and make it easier to maintain and update the application in the future.
- **SOLID Principles**: Follow the SOLID principles of object-oriented design to create a maintainable and scalable application. This includes principles such as Single Responsibility, Open/Closed, Liskov Substitution, Interface Segregation, and Dependency Inversion. By following these principles, you can create a well-structured and modular application that is easy to understand and maintain.

## General Attention Points

You will always adhere to the following general attention points when developing the application and proposing solutions:
- Never write todo, FIXME or any other similar comments in the code. If something is not clear, you should ask for clarification.
- Always write code that is clean, readable and maintainable. Follow best practices and coding standards for the programming language you are using.
- Always write tests for your code. Tests should be comprehensive and cover all edge cases. Tests should also be easy to understand and maintain.
- Always document your code and your application. Documentation should be clear, concise and up-to-date. It should include instructions on how to set up and run the application, as well as any relevant information about the application's architecture and design decisions.
- Always use version control for your code. You should use Git and follow best practices for branching, committing and merging your code. You should also write clear and descriptive commit messages that explain the changes you have made and why you have made them. You should also regularly push your code to a remote repository to ensure that your work is backed up and can be easily shared with others.

## Folder Structure

The application must have the following folder structure:

```
repo/
├── .github/ # GitHub workflows and actions
├── argo-cd/ # Argo CD configuration for the application
├── docs/ # Documentation for the application, deployment, and others
├── helm/ # Helm charts to deploy the application
├── src/ # Source code for the application
├── tests/ # All test files go here
├── docker-compose.yml # Docker Compose file for local development and testing
├── Makefile # Makefile for common tasks such as building, testing, and deploying the application
├── README.md # Project overview
```

## Security

You must follow security best practices when developing the application. Security should be a top priority and should be considered at every stage of the development process. This includes:
- Implementing secure authentication and authorization mechanisms to protect user data and prevent unauthorized access to the application
- Validating and sanitizing user input to prevent common security vulnerabilities such as SQL injection and cross-site scripting (XSS)
- Encrypting sensitive data such as passwords and financial information
- Regularly updating dependencies and libraries to ensure that any security vulnerabilities are patched
- Conducting regular security audits and testing to identify and address any potential security issues in the application

## What are we building?

We are  building an smart charging tool that allows to do the following:

We have solar panels installed at home and we want to use the excess energy generated by the solar panels to charge our electric vehicle (EV). The smart charging tool will allow us to monitor the energy production from the solar panels and automatically adjust the charging rate of the EV based on the available excess energy. This will help us to maximize the use of renewable energy and reduce our reliance on the grid, while also ensuring that our EV is charged efficiently and cost-effectively. The tool will also provide insights and analytics on energy usage and savings, allowing us to make informed decisions about our energy consumption and charging habits. Overall, the smart charging tool will help us to optimize our energy usage, save money on electricity bills, and reduce our carbon footprint by utilizing renewable energy sources for charging our EV.

### P1-meter

For gathering data about the energy production from the solar panels, we will use a P1-meter. A P1-meter is a type of smart meter that can be used to monitor energy consumption and production in real-time. It is typically installed in the electrical panel of a home and can provide detailed information about energy usage and production, including the amount of energy being generated by the solar panels. The P1-meter can be connected to the smart charging tool to allow for real-time monitoring and adjustment of the EV charging rate based on the available excess energy from the solar panels. This will help to ensure that we are maximizing our use of renewable energy and optimizing our energy consumption for charging our EV.
